Text S1 : Cell viability analysis. 23 

The bacteria solution was incubated with different concentrations of R-flurtamone or S-24 

flurtamone (0 -80 μg/L) at 37  °C for 2h . After that, the suspensions were transferred into 25 

a 96 -well plate by adding 10  μL CCK -8 (Aladdin, China) to each well according t o the 26 

manufacturer ’s instructions. They were then incubated at 35 ± 1 °C for 30 min. The plate 27 

absorbance was determined via BioTek ELX800 (BioTek, America) at 450 nm. The 28 

computational formula of relative cell viability was as follows: 29 

Relative cell viabi lity (%) = [OD 450 (exposed flurtamone)/OD 450 (control)]*100 (1) 30 

Where OD 450 (exposed flurtamone) as well as OD 450 (control) are the absorbance at 450 31 

nm of the media samples with or without exposure to  R-flurtamone  or  S-flurtamone. 32 

> 33

Text S2: Agarose gel electrophoresis analysis 34 

Plasmids were extracted from E. coli using the Plasmid Mini KitII (Life Technologies, 35 

USA), Following extraction, agarose gel electrophoresis was applied to verify the presence 36 

of plasmids in each transformant.  10  μL of  plasmids  was mixed with TS -GelRed Nucleic 37 

Acid Gel Stain (Tsingke, Beijing, China), followed by an agarose gel (1.5% w/v) 38 

electrophoresis at 6 V cm −1. The electrophoretograms were finally obtained by using the 39 

Bio -Rad Molcelular Imager FX (Hercules, Canad a). 40 

> 41

Text S3: Cell membrane permeability assay. 42 

The cell membrane permeability was determined by the DNA intercalating fluorescent 43 

dye propidium iodide (PI).  The mixtures of flurtamone enantiomers and  E. coli  cells were 44 

prepared with various concentrations. After incubating at 200 rpm and 25 ℃ for 2 h, the 45 

cell samples were separated from flurtamone  molecules  by centrifugation and washed with 46 

PBS  solution for three times. Then 10  μL of propidium iodide solutio n (1 mg mL -1) was 47 

added to the cell suspensions and incubated in the dark for 20 min, followed by an analysis 48 

of flow cytometry at an exaction wavelength of 488 nm. 49 

> 50

Text S4: Determination of enzyme activity and ATP content .51 

Antioxidative enzymes were extracted from E. coli with small modifications as 52 S-3

described by Gravina et al. 1.Superoxide dismutase (SOD) and catalase (CAT) activities in 53 

E. coli  were measured by a  microplate reader (Thermo Scientific, USA).  Intracellular ATP 54 

levels were measured using the Enhanced ATP Assay Kit (S0027, Beyotime, China), 55 

following the manufacturer's protocol .2-4 After 2 h exposure to  R- or  S-flurtamone, 56 

bacterial suspensions were centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 3 min to collect cells. The pellets 57 

were lysed in 200 μL  of ATP detection reagent lysate and centrifuged (12,000 × g, 5 min, 58 

4 °C). The supernatant was transferred to a 96 -well plate preloaded with an ATP assay 59 

solution containing firefly luciferase and luciferin. The process of bioluminescence 60 

production using  luciferin requires energy from ATP, and its luminescence intensity is 61 

proportional to the ATP concentration. The intracellular ATP content was monitored by a 62 

luminescence microplate reader (Varioskan LUX, USA) .63

enriched_points.expanded_content: Chiral pesticides, such as R-flurtamone, target specific pest species more effectively, minimizing damage to beneficial organisms and reducing overall chemical usage in agriculture.

The application of chiral pesticides raises concerns regarding their persistence in ecosystems, potential for bioaccumulation, and their selective impact on non-target organisms, necessitating careful assessment.

Ongoing research is crucial for optimizing chiral pesticide formulations, improving enantioselectivity, and understanding their environmental interactions to enhance sustainable agricultural practices.
